title: NVIDIA Opens NVLink to Third-Party Silicon with NVLink Fusion | Darshan Jain posted on the topic | LinkedIn
text:
🚀 NVLink Fusion: Why NVIDIA Opened Its Proprietary Interconnect For years, NVLink was NVIDIA’s closed high-speed GPU interconnect — designed to make multiple GPUs behave like one giant accelerator. With Blackwell, NVLink 5 delivers 1.8 TB/s bidirectional bandwidth per GPU. Inside the GB200 NVL72 rack, 72 GPUs are connected in an all-to-all topology, creating an enormous 130 TB/s scale-up fabric. Now Rubin takes it even further. NVLink 6 doubles bandwidth to 3.6 TB/s per GPU, and the Vera Rubin NVL72 rack reaches 260 TB/s aggregate scale-up bandwidth. For AI workloads like Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), where all-to-all communication dominates, interconnect bandwidth is becoming just as critical as raw compute. But the most interesting move is not bandwidth. It’s strategy. NVIDIA is opening NVLink to third-party silicon through “NVLink Fusion.” Custom XPUs can now connect into NVIDIA’s NVLink ecosystem using a UCIe-to-NVLink bridge. That means hyperscalers designing their own AI accelerators can still operate inside NVIDIA’s scale-up fabric. And this changes the game. Amazon has Trainium. Google has TPU. Microsoft has Maia. The industry is also pushing toward open interconnect standards like UALink, backed by AMD, Intel, Google, Microsoft, AWS, and others. So NVIDIA’s response is fascinating: “You don’t have to use our GPUs. But you can still build inside our AI factory architecture.” Through NVLink Fusion, NVIDIA still controls: • The interconnect fabric • The rack-scale architecture • Networking (ConnectX / Spectrum-X) • DPUs (BlueField) • Software ecosystem • System integration standards This is bigger than GPU sales. NVLink Fusion may become NVIDIA’s way of defining the standard architecture for next-generation AI factories — even in systems that don’t exclusively use NVIDIA compute. It looks open. But NVIDIA still writes the rules of that openness. Questions worth thinking about: ❓Will interconnects become more important than the accelerators themselves? ❓Can open standards like UALink realistically challenge NVLink dominance? ❓Is NVIDIA evolving from a GPU company into an AI infrastructure standards company? ❓In future AI clusters, what becomes the real moat: compute, networking, memory, or software? #NVIDIA #NVLink #AIInfrastructure #Semiconductor #VLSI #ChipDesign #AI #GPUs #DataCenter #SiliconPhotonics #Hyperscalers #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #Networking #UCIe #AIClusters #AcceleratedComputing #HardwareEngineering #SystemArchitecture #TechInnovation

      Did you miss the NVIDIA Computex keynote? We got you covered. At GTC Taipei 2026, Jensen Huang walked through Vera Rubin, a new CPU, an open model, an AI PC, and a physical AI stack. Moor Insights & Strategy reads the keynote as one argument: NVIDIA is no longer a chip company. It is a vertically integrated AI platform, and the moat is the supply chain, not the chip. Start with Vera Rubin in full production. Jensen said the supply chain built for it is "twice as large as Grace Blackwell," spanning all 40,000 engineers. Consensus reads a sold-out launch as uncapped demand. Our read is different. Patrick Moorhead called the wall first, noting on November 5 that NVIDIA was "likely sold out of everything it can produce through the end of 2026." Anshel Sag logged the rack math on January 30: 3.5x training, 5x inference versus Blackwell. The constraint is power, packaging, and HBM4 allocation, not orders. The next earnings call is an allocation story, and whoever controls the supply chain prices the cycle. Vera, the CPU, is the tell. "CPU for agents," 88 cores, the highest IPC in the world. Where Wall Street still files NVIDIA under GPU company, Patrick said on March 15 that NVIDIA "has been methodically rewriting what kind of company it is, one conference at a time. The CPU is taking center stage." Matthew Kimball Kimball's line on NUVACORE, April 24, lands the point: "the CPU is wrong for agentic AI." Vera answers that in-house. It is not built to beat Intel or AMD. It is built to disintermediate them inside the rack. Nemotron 3 Ultra and the Open Shell look like a developer gift. Our read is that openness is an antitrust escape hatch. Anshel pre-called the play on April 10 with the open NemoClaw stack. Jason Andersen pushes back where consensus nods: published research shows "attack success rates above 85%," so safety must be "earned, not asserted." The real prize 12 months out is the agentic control plane, and NVIDIA wants the open model and the governance layer both. RTX Spark on N1X gets framed as an x86 fight. We disagree. Apple is the foil. Unified memory plus an Arm CPU on 3nm is the M-series formula. Anshel's October 20 DGX Spark hands-on maps it 1:1, and Patrick called it at GTC 2025: "how to say you're coming after Apple without saying it." By 2027 the premium AI PC looks like a Mac, and it is not one. On physical AI, the crowd chases humanoids. Cosmos 3 is the platform play. Bill Curtis named the real margin at CES 2025: a fourth embodiment beyond agentic, self-driving, and humanoid, the industrial digital-twin factory, not the dancing biped. NVIDIA now owns the CPU, GPU, rack, open stack, agentic shell, physical AI foundation, and supply chain. The 12 to 24 month tension is vertical control against open-ecosystem optics. By 2027 the vertical play wins, and the optics become the fight. To view or add a comment, sign in

      In 2024 NVIDIA built the most powerful compact supercomputer ever put into a single unit. 128 gigabytes of unified memory. 900 gigabytes per second across NVLink-C2C. Grace-Blackwell architecture that eliminated the bandwidth bottleneck between CPU and GPU that has constrained AI inference for a decade. The DGX Spark is not a GPU. It is a sovereign intelligence substrate. The question nobody was asking loudly enough was what governance architecture is worthy of running on it. Most software treats hardware as a commodity. Any sufficiently capable model on any sufficiently powerful chip. The hardware is infrastructure. The software is the product. AFS Cortex inverts that completely. The 900 gigabytes per second NVLink-C2C link is not a performance feature in AFS Cortex. It is a constitutional requirement. Without that specific throughput the Strategic Advantage Window for real-time governance over the Knowledge Graph collapses and the constitutional veto arrives too late to matter. The system is not optimised for Grace-Blackwell. It is predicated on it. The sm_121 architecture is not a compatibility target. The Outlier-Preserving Quantisation logic is surgically tuned to sm_121 Tensor Cores in a way that renders the governance kernels physically incompatible with competitor silicon. This is not a configuration choice. It is the architecture. The result is a system that does not just run on the DGX Spark. It is defined by it.What this means commercially is significant. Every enterprise deploying AFS Cortex is deploying sovereign hardware. The intelligence is fixed-asset, not cloud-subscribed. The governance is constitutional, not policy-based. The audit trail is cryptographic, not logged. And the entire stack is physically bound to NVIDIA Blackwell silicon in a way that cannot be replicated on any other hardware. In May 2026 the internal certification programme completed. All governance tests passed. The constitutional veto was recorded live at 0.088 milliseconds. The system completed 1,077 autonomous cycles in one hour with zero errors and zero human interventions. The DGX Spark is the most capable sovereign AI substrate available today. AFS Cortex is the governance architecture that makes it deployable in the regulated enterprise markets that have been waiting for exactly this combination. NVIDIA built the hardware that makes this possible. We built the governance architecture that makes it defensible. That conversation is worth having.       Nvidia just decided that owning the GPU market wasn't enough and is now coming for the entire PC silicon conversation with the RTX Spark. The RTX Spark is Nvidia's move into compact, high-performance PC chips designed to compete directly with Apple Silicon in the thin-and-light segment. This is significant because Apple has spent years making the M-series the default benchmark for efficient, powerful personal computing, and no x86 or discrete GPU player has meaningfully challenged that narrative at the chip architecture level until now. Nvidia bringing its GPU architecture DNA into an integrated PC chip form factor changes the competitive framing entirely, especially for workloads like local AI inference, rendering, and model fine-tuning where raw GPU efficiency matters most. The longer play here is about who controls the local AI compute layer. Apple locked in creatives and developers with M-series performance per watt. If Nvidia can match or beat that while adding CUDA ecosystem compatibility, the developer stickiness shifts dramatically. Most local AI tooling is still built around CUDA, and that gives Nvidia a structural advantage Apple cannot easily replicate. The PC is quietly becoming a serious AI compute device again, and Nvidia wants to own that transition from the silicon up. If local AI compute becomes the new battleground, does Nvidia's CUDA ecosystem advantage outweigh Apple's years of silicon optimization momentum. To view or add a comment, sign in

      The memory market has recently been shrouded in negative rumors regarding Nvidia's halving of memory module usage in Vera Rubin server racks. However, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has refuted these rumors, stating that "there is no end in sight to the memory shortage," and announced plans to purchase more memory from SK Hynix. The two companies will also collaborate on developing next-generation memory technology. Analysts point out that Huang's remarks have dispelled the rumors of a sharp reduction in Nvidia's memory usage, providing reassurance to memory manufacturers such as Nanya Technology and Winbond. With Nvidia purchasing more SK Hynix high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and working together to develop next-generation technology, Taiwanese companies, particularly TSMC's subsidiary SK Hynix, will be the biggest winners, as they continue to develop HBM4-related ASIC IP in cooperation with SK Hynix. Jensen Huang pointed out that the current demand in the AI industry far exceeds supply. The entire supply chain, from wafer manufacturing and advanced packaging to silicon photonics technology, is experiencing a supply shortage, with demand so high it's difficult to meet. He predicts this situation will continue for many years. Furthermore, AI infrastructure is still in a phase of rapid expansion, and the global investment in data centers shows no signs of cooling down. Regarding rumors that Nvidia has halved its use of memory modules in Vera Rubin server racks, Huang refuted them, emphasizing that "there is no end in sight to the memory shortage." Huang stated that Nvidia purchases billions of dollars worth of memory from SK Hynix annually, and this year's purchases will increase significantly. Nvidia also announced a multi-year technology cooperation agreement with SK Hynix to jointly advance next-generation memory solutions needed for global AI data centers. He emphasized that SK Hynix will continue to be Nvidia's largest partner, and the two companies have secured a long-term agreement, expanding their cooperation from AI supercomputers to CPUs, PCs, and robotics. Nvidia's expanded procurement of SK Hynix memory and collaboration in developing next-generation memory technology will greatly benefit Creative Labs. Creative Labs continues to invest in HBM4-related ASIC-side IP. The market anticipates that as Nvidia and others actively adopt HBM4 in conjunction with subsequent products, it will likely drive cloud computing giants to gradually follow suit with their self-developed chips. Creative Labs' early investment in related IP will be advantageous in securing more projects. To view or add a comment, sign in

However, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has refuted these rumors, stating that "there is no end in sight to the memory shortage," and announced plans to purchase more memory from SK Hynix. The two companies will also collaborate on developing next-generation memory technology. Analysts point out that Huang's remarks dispel the rumors of a sharp reduction in Nvidia's memory usage, providing reassurance to memory manufacturers such as Nanya Technology and Winbond regarding their future prospects. NVIDIA plans to purchase more SK Hynix high-bandwidth memory (HBM) and collaborate to accelerate next-generation technology development. Among Taiwanese manufacturers, TSMC's investment in Creative Labs, in collaboration with SK Hynix to continue developing HBM4-related ASIC-side IP, will be a major winner. Huang Renxun pointed out that the current demand in the AI industry far exceeds supply. The supply chain, from wafer manufacturing and advanced packaging to silicon photonics technology, is experiencing a supply shortage. Demand is so high that it is difficult to meet, and this situation is expected to continue for many years. Furthermore, AI infrastructure is still in a phase of rapid expansion, and the global investment in data centers shows no signs of cooling down. Regarding rumors that NVIDIA had halved its Vera Rubin server rack memory module usage, Jensen Huang refuted them, emphasizing that "there is no end in sight to the memory shortage." Huang stated that NVIDIA purchases billions of dollars worth of memory from SK Hynix annually, and this year's purchases will increase significantly. NVIDIA also announced a multi-year technology cooperation agreement with SK Hynix to jointly advance next-generation memory solutions for global AI data centers. He stressed that SK Hynix will continue to be NVIDIA's largest partner, and the two companies have secured a long-term agreement, expanding their cooperation from AI supercomputers to CPUs, PCs, and robotics. NVIDIA's increased procurement of SK Hynix memory and collaboration in developing next-generation memory technology will greatly benefit Creative Labs. Creative Labs continues to develop HBM4-related ASIC-side IP. The market expects that as long as NVIDIA and others actively adopt HBM4 in their products, it will drive major cloud computing companies to gradually follow suit with their self-developed chips.       Why NVIDIA’s Ultimate Weapon May be the CPU While the tech world focuses on Blackwell GPUs, NVIDIA is pulling off a quiet architectural heist. The most critical component of their trillion-dollar future? It’s not the GPU. It’s the CPU. Most analysts still treat CPUs as a commodity afterthought. NVidia treats it as the ultimate strategic leverage. In the AI era, the greatest threat to ROI isn't a lack of raw horsepower—it's idle horsepower. Consider the math of data center efficiency: A single high-end AI factory rack costs roughly $3 million. If that rack runs at 70% utilization due to data bottlenecks, it wastes $900,000 in stranded capital per rack.Across a hyperscaler deploying 10,000 racks, that’s a staggering $9 billion in destroyed value. This is why the Grace CPU exists. It wasn't built to compete with Intel or AMD on traditional workloads; it was engineered to eliminate the systemic friction that keeps GPUs waiting for data. Look at how the integration scales: A single GB200 NVL72 system tightly couples 72 Blackwell GPUs with 36 Grace CPUs. Each Grace CPU pumps out 960 GB/s of memory bandwidth utilizing 480 GB of ultra-fast memory. This cohesive unit delivers 1.4 exaflops of AI performance, treating hundreds of discrete chips as a single, massive compute domain via NVLink. By controlling the CPU, NVIDIA controls memory movement, workload orchestration, and telemetry. With global AI infrastructure spend projected to top $2.1 trillion by 2030, a mere 5% to 10% gain in data center utilization translates to $105B – $210B in unlocked economic value. That economic leverage is a software-like margin realized on hardware scale. The Bottom Line The AI infrastructure race is no longer about who builds the fastest silicon. It’s about who controls the entire system. By verticalizing the architecture from the CPU to the network switch, NVIDIA hasn't just built a better chip—they’ve built an ecosystem where competing on individual GPU benchmarks is entirely irrelevant.       NVIDIA Vera is in production Unlike Intel and AMD, which offer their processors in numerous models to cover all market segments, NVIDIA has made a radical choice: a single Vera SKU. The goal is not to conquer the overall CPU market, but to meet a very specific need: workloads related to artificial intelligence and high-performance computing. https://lnkd.in/ejqnaatc To view or add a comment, sign in 
Uh? Was it "closed"? I seem to recall IBM and OpenPOWER using NVLink for CPU to GPU communications over 10 years ago. That was the world's first GPU powered supercomputer. I'm assuming much of the NVLink topology and protocol was "open". Otherwise I'm not sure how they would have developed this computer all. But even making it "open" doesn't solve the physical connectivity issues. NVLink is a proprietary bus protocol. I like to think of it like NVMe for GPUs. It treats a GPU like a co-processor with coherent cache and shared HBM / CPU memory hierarchy. This model is required to achieve effective data movement between memory cpu and gpu. Today that is done with proprietary NVLink switches -- trays of them in an NVL72 rack. Not exactly "open".
